title: What is R-CNN?
concept: R-CNN (Region-based CNN) is an object detection framework that uses Selective Search to propose regions, crops them, and runs each crop through a CNN.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a region proposal algorithm like Selective Search output?

options.text: About 2,000 candidate bounding boxes of interest

exercises.explain: Selective Search outputs candidate coordinates (proposals) that are likely to contain objects.

cards.title: Cropping and Scanning
cards.body: Imagine you have a large photo of a crowd and you want to find faces. Instead of looking at the whole photo, you take a pair of scissors, cut out 2,000 tiny rectangular sections where you think faces might be, and then look at each cropped snippet one by one. That is the core idea of R-CNN.

cards.title: R-CNN
cards.body: R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network) is a pioneering object detection framework. It generates region proposals using Selective Search, crops and warps those regions to a fixed size, and feeds each region crop separately through a CNN to extract features.

cards.title: The Magnifying Glass
cards.body: Instead of scanning the whole painting at once, you use a sliding frame to isolate specific parts. You examine each isolated section under a magnifying glass, analyzing and labeling what you see in that small frame before moving to the next one.

cards.title: The Original R-CNN Paper
cards.body: Introduced in 2014, R-CNN dramatically outperformed traditional methods on the Pascal VOC dataset. It proved that deep features extracted by CNNs (like AlexNet) could yield high-accuracy localization when combined with proposal regions.

cards.title: Not a single forward pass
cards.body: It's a myth that R-CNN looks at the whole image and outputs boxes. R-CNN actually crops the image 2,000 times and runs the CNN 2,000 separate times! This makes it extremely slow during inference.

cards.title: R-CNN Pipeline Flow
cards.body: Visualizing how R-CNN processes an image step-by-step:

left.text:
Input image -> Selective Search
generates 2,000 candidate crops.

right.text:
Each of the 2,000 crops is warped
and passed through the CNN.

exercises.explain: R-CNN stands for Region-based Convolutional Neural Network, highlighting its focus on region proposals.

exercises.prompt: True or False: R-CNN extracts features from the entire image at once in a single CNN forward pass.
exercises.explain: False. R-CNN runs a separate CNN forward pass for each of the 2,000 proposed region crops, which makes it very computationally expensive.
Selective Search is the CPU-based algorithm used in R-CNN to generate candidate bounding boxes.
exercises.sentence: R-CNN uses ___ to generate candidate region proposals before the CNN runs.

exercises.prompt: Which drawback is associated with the original R-CNN architecture?
exercises.explain: Running the CNN 2,000 times per image is R-CNN's primary speed bottleneck.

options.text: It requires running the CNN feature extractor 2,000 times per image
It can only detect single objects

exercises.prompt: You are explaining R-CNN to a colleague. They ask why R-CNN is so slow. What is the main reason you should give them?

options.text: Because it runs the CNN independently 2,000 times for 2,000 separate overlapping crops, wasting computation.
Because linear SVMs are too complex to compute on a GPU.
Because it doesn't use data augmentation during training.
Because it converts all pixel coordinates to binary values.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The redundant feature computation for 2,000 crops is the main cause of the R-CNN slowdown. Next: let's study the specific pipeline steps of R-CNN!
